notes: Remains of Ainu fortifications on the Nemuro Peninsula.
Western-style star fort. Site of the Battle of Hakodate in 1868.
The only traditional Japanese-style castle in Hokkaido.
One of 12 surviving original keeps. Famous for cherry blossoms.
Seat of the Nanbu clan in Iwate.
Aoba Castle, built by Date Masamune.
Ancient provincial headquarters built in the Nara period (724).
Ancient provincial fortress. Also known as Dewa Fortress.
Also called Kasumi Castle.
Seat of the Uesugi clan.
Seat of the Katakura clan. Timber-reconstructed keep.
Also called Tsuruga Castle. Site of the Boshin War in 1868.
Seat of the Mito Tokugawa, one of the Three Gosanke.
Residence of the Ashikaga clan, founded by Minamoto no Yoshikuni.
Earthwork castle built by Nagano Narimasa.
Mountain castle of the Nitta-Iwamatus clan.
Seat of Hojo Ujikuni.
Built by Ota Dokan. The honmaru palace survives.
Seat of the Hotta clan.
Seat of the Tokugawa shogunate. Now the Imperial Palace.
Mountain stronghold of Hojo Ujiteru. Scene of a tragic fall.
Base of the Hojo clan. Besieged by Toyotomi Hideyoshi in 1590.
Stone castle built after the fall of the Takeda clan.
National Treasure. One of 12 surviving keeps. Also called Karasu-jo.
Built by Sanada Masayuki. Repelled Tokugawa forces twice.
Laid out by Yamamoto Kansuke. Sits lower than its castle town.
Seat of the Mizoguchi clan. Famous for its three shachi ornaments.
Mountain stronghold of Uesugi Kenshin. Considered impregnable.
One of 12 surviving keeps. Also called Kasumi Castle.
Base of the Asakura clan. The castle town ruins are a Special Historic Site.
Impregnable mountain castle of the Hatakeyama clan.
Seat of the Maeda clan, lords of the million-koku Kaga domain.
One of the three great mountain castles of Etchu province.
Retirement castle of Maeda Toshiie.
One of Japan's three great mountain castles. At 717m elevation.
Seat of Oda Nobunaga. Originally called Inabayama Castle.
Retirement castle of Tokugawa Ieyasu as Ogosho (retired shogun).
Japan's first timber-reconstructed castle tower.
Famous for its barrier moats (shoji-bori), built by the Hojo clan.
Famous for its golden shachi ornaments. Built by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
National Treasure. Surviving keep overlooking the Kiso River.
Site of the Battle of Nagashino (1575).
Birthplace of Tokugawa Ieyasu.
Also called Toyohashi Castle. Sits beside the Toyo River.
Famous for its high stone walls. Renovated by Todo Takatora.
Built by Gamo Ujisato.
National Treasure. Seat of the Ii clan. Home of the Hikone Cat mascot.
Seat of Oda Nobunaga. Japan's first full-scale tenshu.
UNESCO World Heritage Site. Built by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1603.
Built by Toyotomi Hideyoshi. Symbol of Osaka.
Mountain castle of Kusunoki Masashige. At the foot of Mt. Kongo.
UNESCO World Heritage Site and National Treasure. Japan's largest surviving castle complex.
Built by Tokugawa Ieyasu to keep western lords in check.
Seat of the Ogasawara clan. Now Akashi Park.
Seat of the Asano clan. Setting of the Chushingura (47 Ronin) story.
The 'Sky Castle'. Famous for its stone walls rising above the sea of clouds.
Seat of Toyotomi Hidenaga.
Mountain castle built by the Akiyama clan.
Built by Toyotomi Hidenaga. Seat of the Kishu Tokugawa clan.
Site of Toyotomi Hideyoshi's famous 'Tottori Starvation Siege'.
National Treasure. Also called Chidori Castle. One of 12 surviving keeps.
Impregnable mountain stronghold of the Amago clan.
Mountain castle in the 'Little Kyoto of the West'.
The highest-elevation surviving castle keep in Japan.
Built by Mori Tadamasa. Famous for its cherry blossoms.
Ancient Korean-style mountain fortress, built around 700 AD.
Built by Mizuno Katsushige.
Built by the Mori clan. Destroyed by the atomic bomb in 1945.
Floating castle built by Kobayakawa Takakage.
Seat of the Mori clan. Birthplace of the Meiji Restoration.
Mountain castle of the Kikkawa clan. Famous for Kintai Bridge.
Seat of the Hachisuka clan.
One of 12 surviving keeps. Famous for its massive stone walls.
Seaside castle. Also called Tamamo Castle.
Coastal castle built by Todo Takatora.
One of 12 surviving keeps. Stands atop a hill in the city center.
One of 12 surviving keeps. Seat of the Date clan in Iyo province.
One of 12 surviving keeps. The honmaru palace also survives.
Seat of the Kuroda clan. Also called Maizuru Castle.
Ancient Korean-style mountain fortress built in 665 AD.
Built by Toyotomi Hideyoshi as a base for his Korean invasions.
Yayoi-period moated settlement. One of the origins of Japanese fortifications.
Seat of the Matsuura clan. Beautiful contrast of sea and castle.
Built by the Matsukura clan. Site of the Shimabara Rebellion.
Built by Kato Kiyomasa. Also called Ginnan Castle. Considered impregnable.
Riverside castle. The Sagara clan ruled here for 700 years.
Built by Fukuhara Naotaka.
Setting for 'Kojo no Tsuki' (Moon over the Ruined Castle). Beautiful stone walls.
Seat of the Ito clan. Called the 'Little Kyoto of Kyushu'.
Branch castle of the Shimazu clan.
Seat of the Shimazu clan. Site of the Seinan War (1877).
Mountain castle of the Kimo-tsuki clan. Famous for its samurai residences.
UNESCO World Heritage Site. Seat of the Northern Ryukyu king.
UNESCO World Heritage Site. Castle of Gosamaru.
UNESCO World Heritage Site. Royal palace of the Ryukyu Kingdom. Partially destroyed by fire in 2019.
Seat of the Oda clan in Hitachi.
Seat of the Satake clan. An earthwork castle with no stone walls.
Seat of the Asano clan. Setting of the Chushingura (47 Ronin) story.
Built by Yuki Hideyasu.
Famous for cherry blossoms. Branch castle of the Takeda clan.
One of only two Western-style star forts in Japan.
Also called Kaizu Castle. Strategic point in the Kawanakajima battles.
Mountain castle on a rocky peak above the Kiso River.
Timber-reconstructed castle. Associated with Yamauchi Kazutoyo's loyal wife.
Birthplace of Mori Ranmaru.
Site of fierce fighting during the Battle of Komaki-Nagakute.
'Castle of Success' where Tokugawa Ieyasu ruled for 17 years.
Contested between the Takeda and Tokugawa clans.
Also called Baba Castle. Built by Takeda Shingen.
The castle where Hojo Soun rose to power.
One of the Hojo clan's main bases.
Siege fortification built by Toyotomi Hideyoshi during the Odawara Campaign.
Impregnable mountain stronghold of Hojo Ujiteru.
Seat of the Hatakeyama clan.
Mountain castle in the Hiki hills.
Branch castle of the Hojo clan.
Mountain castle of the Naito clan.
One of the seven great castles of the Kanto region.
Built by Sanada Masayuki. Its seizure triggered the Odawara Campaign.
Natural fortress of the Sanada clan.
Castle of the Sanada clan. Key stronghold in Kozuke province.
Castle of the Sanada clan. Seat of their Numata domain.
Seat of the Nagao clan, retainers of the Uesugi.
Honjo of the Nitta clan.
Seat of the Tamura clan.
Also called Kasumi Castle. Famous for its chrysanthemum doll festival.
Three-story corner turret reconstructed in timber.
Mountain castle of the Ashina clan.
Castle in the city of Odate.
Seat of the Ando clan.
Castle of the Ashina clan. Famous for its samurai residences.
Seat of the Rusu clan.
Castle where Date Masamune moved before Sendai.
Castle of a retainer of the Date clan.
Built by Nanbu Moroyuki. The central enclosure has been reconstructed.
Seat of the Kitabatake clan.
Site of the Kunohe Rebellion (1591).
Ancient provincial fortress of the Heian period (802).
Seat of the Rusu clan.
Castle of the Kuroishi domain, a branch of the Tsugaru clan.
Seat of the Uesugi clan. (Zoku 100 entry, slightly offset from No.10)
Site of the Mogami-Uesugi clash, concurrent with the Battle of Sekigahara.
Castle of the Mogami clan.
Mountain castle of the Honjo clan.
Site of the succession dispute after Uesugi Kenshin's death.
Castle of Matsudaira Tadateru, sixth son of Tokugawa Ieyasu.
Seat of Naoe Kanetsugu.
Built by Maeda Toshinori.
Seat of Oda Nobunaga. (Zoku 100 entry, slightly offset from No.48)
Site of a fierce siege concurrent with the Battle of Sekigahara.
The first castle where Toyotomi Hideyoshi served as lord.
Strategic post on the Tokaido highway.
Seat of Toyotomi Hidetsugu.
Built by Akechi Mitsuhide.
Also called Oka Castle.
Mountain castle of Akechi Mitsuhide.
Seat of Hosokawa Fujitaka. Akechi Mitsuhide's daughter was married here.
Seat of Toyotomi Hidenaga. (Zoku 100 entry, offset from No.57)
One of Japan's three great mountain castles.
Seat of the Mizuno clan.
Famous for the Kishiwada Danjiri Festival.
Seat of Miyoshi Nagayoshi.
Mountain castle that served as Miyoshi Nagayoshi's main base.
Seat of Takayama Ukon.
Awaji Island castle of the Atagi clan.
Seat of the Wakisaka clan.
Mountain castle of the Ogino and Saito clans.
Mountain castle of Yamana Suketoshi in Tajima.
Seat of the Yamana clan. In the 'Little Kyoto of Tajima'.
Mountain castle of the Akamatsu clan.
Also called Wakasa Onigajo.
Mountain castle of the Yamana clan in Tajima.
Also called Minato-yama Castle.
Built by Furuta Shigenari.
Seat of the Mitoya clan.
Original main castle of the Kobayakawa clan.
Site of Hashiba Hideyoshi's famous water-flood siege.
Castle of the Akamatsu clan. Also called Kozuki Castle.
Built by Ukita Hideie. Also called Karasu-jo (Crow Castle).
Seat of the Mori clan. (Zoku 100 entry, offset from No.70)
Mountain castle of the Kikkawa clan. (Zoku 100 entry, offset from No.71)
Seat of the Hachisuka clan. (Zoku 100 entry, offset from No.72)
The first seat of the Ikoma clan when they entered Sanuki province.
Branch castle of Ozu Castle.
Timber-reconstructed castle tower. Stands beside the Hiji River.
Mountain castle of the Saionji clan.
Main castle of the Chosokabe clan.
Seat of the Ichijo clan. Known as the 'Little Kyoto of Tosa'.
Built by Hosokawa Tadaoki. Famous for its unique roof (karazukuri) style.
Castle of the Aso clan.
Seat of the Arima clan.
Seat of the Nabeshima clan. The honmaru palace has been reconstructed.
Built by Terazawa Hirotaka. Also called Maizuru Castle.
Mountain castle with beautiful stone walls. (Zoku 100 entry, offset from No.88)
Built by Takahashi Mototane.
Main castle of the Ito clan.
Linked-enclosure mountain castle in Osumi province.
Mountain castle of the Iriki clan.
UNESCO World Heritage Site. Gusuku built by Gosamaru.
UNESCO World Heritage Site. Gusuku of Amawari.
